title: Kaitiakitanga o Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku / Guardianship of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream
subject: Health & Physical Education
culturalContext: Students will explore the health of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, a significant stream that flows through our rohe and holds cultural importance to Ngāti Kahungunu. The stream's name references the kōtukutuku (tree fuchsia) that once flourished along its banks, and its waters have sustained our community for generations. Students will connect with local kaumātua who share pūrākau about the awa's significance and learn about current conservation efforts led by Ngāti Kahungunu environmental kaitiaki.
learningObjectives: Measure and analyze water quality data from three different sites along Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku using scientific testing kits
Explain how the mauri (life force) of the awa connects to the physical and mental wellbeing of our community through specific examples
Design and implement a practical kaitiakitanga action that addresses an identified issue affecting the health of the awa
Present findings and recommendations to community stakeholders using appropriate cultural protocols and scientific evidence

activities.title: Whakawhanaungatanga ki te Awa
activities.description: Students visit Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku with local kaumātua who share pūrākau (stories) of the stream's significance. Students perform karakia and learn proper protocols for engaging with the waterway while observing its current state and surrounding environment.

activities.title: Titiro, Whakarongo, Kōrero - Environmental Health Assessment
activities.description: Using water testing kits, students collect and analyze data from three strategic points along the awa (upstream, mid-stream, and near confluence). They measure pH, nitrate levels, turbidity, and temperature while recording observations of flora, fauna, and human impacts.

activities.title: Hui with Ngāti Kahungunu Kaitiaki
activities.description: Students interview local environmental guardians from Ngāti Kahungunu's environmental team to understand current conservation initiatives, challenges facing the awa, and how water health connects to community wellbeing through traditional and contemporary perspectives.

activities.title: Mahi Kaitiaki - Practical Action Project
activities.description: Based on their findings, students design and implement a practical conservation action such as planting native riparian vegetation, creating educational signage for the community, organizing a stream clean-up event, or developing resources about preventing pollution.

activities.title: Whakatau - Recommendations and Presentation
activities.description: Students prepare and present their findings, data analysis, and conservation recommendations to community stakeholders using both scientific evidence and cultural frameworks, concluding with a shared kai prepared using traditional methods.

resources: Local kaumātua and Ngāti Kahungunu kaitiaki experts
Freshwater testing kits (pH strips, nitrate tests, thermometers, turbidity tubes)
Hawke's Bay Regional Council's 'State of Our Waters' online monitoring platform

tasks: Water quality data collection and analysis report
Documented interview with community kaitiaki including key insights
Implementation of practical kaitiakitanga action with photographic evidence
Formal presentation to community stakeholders using appropriate cultural protocols

nzcAlignment: Health and Physical Education: A3 - Safety management; B3 - Interpersonal skills; C3 - Community resources; D3 - Societal attitudes and values
Science: Nature of Science - Investigating in science; Planet Earth and Beyond - Earth systems
Social Studies: Continuity and Change; Place and Environment
